message.content: {"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.48},{"player":"RABBIT","probability":0.42},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["CHICKEN and RABBIT contributed the maximum (20) in every round (6/6), matching Evelyn’s steady, reliable high contributions and preference to model cooperative norms rather than punish.","GORILLA contributed in 5 of 6 rounds (single lapse), consistent with Evelyn’s tendency to mostly cooperate and forgive occasional lapses.","Selected players show persistent cooperation even when others defect in some rounds, aligning with Evelyn’s long-term focus on collective welfare and trust in others."],"uncertainty_notes":["The game provides only binary contribution choices and no explicit punishment/reward actions, so inferred motives (forgiveness, norm-modeling) are based on contribution patterns rather than direct behavioral signals.","There is minimal chat (one message from PARROT) and no private reasoning statements; players’ internal motivations may differ from their revealed contributions.","Several players (SLOTH, DUCK, DOG) also show high cooperation (5/6), so distinguishing among similarly cooperative types is imprecise; probabilities reflect this uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"DOG","probability":0.25},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH contributed 20 every round and never punished others; minimal chat and a cordial sign-off fit Evelyn’s steady, non-punitive cooperative style.","DOG contributed 20 every round and shows no recorded punishment actions; chat indicates trust and mild norm-enforcement via persuasion rather than costly punishment, matching Evelyn’s preference to model cooperation.","PARROT contributed 20 every round and has no recorded punishments; low visibility but consistent high contribution aligns with Evelyn’s steady, welfare-focused behavior."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe contributions, punishments, and public chat — internal motives (e.g., altruism vs. strategic cooperation) are not observable, so alignment is inferential.","Some players used small punishments inconsistently; Evelyn might occasionally use small costly incentives rather than punish, but the dataset has no explicit costly reward actions to observe.","Display/reporting inconsistencies (especially around OWL) and limited chat content for some players (e.g., PARROT) introduce uncertainty in matching behavioral style beyond contributions and punishment records."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.7},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently contributed the maximum (20) nearly every round (matches Evelyn’s steady high contributions).","Regularly used small, costly rewards directed at many peers to sustain cooperation rather than punishing (frequent 1-unit rewards across rounds).","Pro-social chat messages promoting contribution and modeling cooperative norm (e.g. advocating max contribution).","Rarely if ever used large punitive transfers; emphasis on rewarding contributors rather than policing aggressively."],"uncertainty_notes":["Multiple players (e.g., GORILLA, DUCK, OWL, RABBIT) also contributed very consistently and gave rewards, so distinguishing subtle motivational differences from behavior alone is uncertain.","Chat comments from CHICK sometimes signal conditionality (‘leechers get nothing’), which could indicate a more policing stance than Evelyn’s described forgiveness—behavioral signals are mixed.","Observations are limited to revealed contributions, rewards, and short chat lines; internal motives (duty-driven generosity vs. strategic signaling) cannot be observed directly."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.65},{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.23},{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.12}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH consistently contributed the full 20 every round, regularly encouraged cooperation in chat, and mostly used small positive rewards while only occasionally issuing minor punishments — matching Evelyn’s steady high contributions, norm‑modeling, and rare use of small sanctions.","CHICK frequently gave many small +1 rewards to other players and encouraged group cooperation in chat, indicating a tendency to sustain cooperation via costly rewards and to avoid harsh punishment — consistent with Evelyn’s use of small rewards and forgiveness (though CHICK’s early contribution record is mixed).","MOOSE repeatedly paid small +1 rewards to many players (appearing to support and reinforce contributors) and sometimes engaged helpfully in chat, matching Evelyn’s tendency to sustain cooperation through small costly rewards, but MOOSE’s repeated low contributions make them a weaker match for Evelyn’s reliably high contributions."],"uncertainty_notes":["We infer motives from observed contributions, punishment/reward patterns, and chat, but internal motivations are not directly observable — different behaviors (e.g., rewarding vs contributing) trade off in the match.","Some players mix cooperative and noncooperative actions across rounds; assignment reflects which pattern best fits Evelyn’s core traits (steady high contribution, rare punishment, occasional small rewards).","Exact probabilities are approximate judgments given limited information and possible role changes over rounds."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.65},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.25},{"player":"COW","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["GORILLA: Mostly contributed high amounts after an early modest contribution and used many small costly positive rewards (round 16 and earlier) to reinforce cooperation — closely matches Evelyn’s tendency to give steadily and occasionally use small rewards rather than punish.","PARROT: Consistently contributed the maximum every round and publicly encouraged others, modeling cooperative norms rather than policing — aligns with Evelyn’s steady, trustful cooperation and norm-building.","COW: After an early low contribution, COW quickly committed to maxing and frequently posted supportive chat messages — plausible match for Evelyn’s willingness to forgive lapses and promote group welfare."],"uncertainty_notes":["We observe only contributions, public chat, and a few reward actions; private motives and occasional misclicks (players apologised) could explain some deviations.","Rewards are infrequent in this game, so absence of rewarding behavior (e.g., for PARROT or COW) doesn’t prove they wouldn’t sometimes use small costly rewards.","Some players’ one-off low contributions may reflect mistakes rather than true preferences, which introduces uncertainty when inferring steady moral motivations."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.62},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.24},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.14}],"behavioral_basis":["CHICKEN: Frequently gives full or high contributions in many rounds, sends small positive rewards broadly (including rewarding many players in round 4), posts encouraging chat (“good job everyone”) — matches Evelyn’s steady pro-social contributions, tendency to model norms and use small costly rewards rather than punish.","DUCK: Consistently and repeatedly uses small costly rewards for many players across rounds (including a large 3-unit reward to SLOTH), showing the willingness to spend private payoff to sustain cooperation. While DUCK’s early contributions are uneven, the strong pro-reward behavior aligns with Evelyn’s described strategy of occasionally using rewards to sustain cooperation.","GORILLA: Moves toward consistently high contributions over the game and occasionally gives small positive rewards (e.g., to SLOTH). Shows forgiving/cooperative pattern and eventual steady high contribution, consistent with Evelyn’s tendency to trust and maintain cooperation, though GORILLA is a less active rewarder than CHICKEN or DUCK."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral inferences are based only on observed contributions, reward transfers, and chat; internal motivations can’t be observed directly.","Players’ patterns change over time (e.g., some start low then become reliable), so early-round deviations could reflect experimentation rather than stable preference.","Rewards are anonymous in summaries and we don’t see who individual players saw when deciding; attribution of motive (e.g., signaling vs. sincere preference for group welfare) is therefore uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.65},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.20},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently high contributions (20 each round) — matches Evelyn’s steady, selfless giving.","Minimal or no use of punishment (GORILLA) — aligns with Evelyn’s tendency to trust and rarely punish.","Occasional small punishments by PARROT and some small rewards/punishments by DUCK — plausible partial matches to Evelyn’s occasional use of small costly rewards and low propensity to police.","Active, large-scale punishment by SLOTH and heavy back-and-forth policing by DOG/CHICKEN conflict with Evelyn’s forgiving, norm-modeling profile, so they were not chosen."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe choices and punishment/reward magnitudes, not motives — similar actions (e.g., rewarding vs punishing) can reflect different intentions (norm-building vs retaliation).","Some players (DUCK, PARROT) show mixed behavior across rounds (both rewards and punishments), making attribution to a single stable motive uncertain.","GORILLA’s lack of visible sanctions fits Evelyn well, but absence of reward use (Evelyn sometimes rewards) leaves small uncertainty in the match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.6},{"player":"CROCODILE","probability":0.3},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent high contributions overall (GORILLA contributed 20 in 13/14 rounds; CROCODILE contributed 20 in all rounds) — matches Evelyn’s steady giving.","Frequent small positive transfers: GORILLA repeatedly gives +1 rewards to many cooperators across rounds, matching Evelyn’s tendency to use small costly rewards to sustain cooperation.","CROCODILE also issues many small rewards (especially late) and models pro-social reinforcement, aligning with Evelyn’s preference to model norms rather than police them.","PARROT gives many small rewards as well, consistent with Evelyn’s reward strategy, but also shows more punitive actions and a single defection, making it a weaker match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inference is based only on observed contributions and punishment/reward events; motives (forgiveness, trust) are not directly observable.","Some players punish in response to repeated defections (retaliatory punishment) — this can look inconsistent with Evelyn’s ‘rarely punishes’ description even if motivated by maintaining cooperation.","A single defection (e.g., GORILLA in round 2, PARROT in round 3) could be noise or a deliberate choice; ranking treats overall patterns rather than isolated lapses."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"DOG","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH: contributed the maximum every round and stayed silent—reflects steady, selfless cooperation and modelling norms rather than policing them.","DOG: consistently contributed and only offered brief positive chat (\"yay\")—matches quiet, reliable pro-social behavior and trust.","GORILLA: consistently contributed and encouraged the group (\"all in guys\", celebratory)—aligns with promoting cooperation through positive modeling rather than punishment."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only public contributions and chat are observable; motives (e.g., accidental clicks, payoff-maximizing intent) are not certain.","Some players had occasional misclicks or brief chat that could mask different underlying strategies.","My mapping emphasizes sustained full contributions and low-punishment communication as key indicators of my persona; other interpretations are possible."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.5},{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.3},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.2}],"behavioral_basis":["High, near-uniform contributions across rounds (matches Evelyn's reliable high contributions).","Minimal use of punishment and explicit chat messages expressing preference for kindness and not punishing (aligns with Evelyn's tendency to model norms rather than police them).","Supportive, community-oriented chat (e.g., expressing shared commitment and saying they will miss the group) and forgiving/lenient remarks (consistent with Evelyn's trust and forgiveness)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Cannot observe private motives—alignment inferred from contributions, punishment actions, and chat only.","Some players (e.g., SLOTH, RABBIT) also show high contributions; choice among similar-high contributors is partly subjective based on chat tone.","No evidence of small costly rewarding actions (Evelyn sometimes uses rewards), so matching relies mainly on contribution and punishment patterns."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DOG","probability":0.4},{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.35},{"player":"WHALE","probability":0.25}],"behavioral_basis":["Contributed 20 in every round (10/10), matching Evelyn’s steady, high-contribution strategy.","Maintained cooperation even when many peers defected in some rounds—consistent with trusting, forgiving, norm-modeling behavior rather than policing.","No observed punishment/reward actions in this game; selection based on pure contribution consistency and willingness to forgo private payoff."],"uncertainty_notes":["Several other players (FROG also contributed 10/10; OWL and COW contributed 9/10) are nearly indistinguishable on contribution frequency, so assignment among perfect contributors is uncertain.","The game only records binary contributions (0 or 20) and provides no info on motivations, punishments, or small costly rewards Evelyn might use, so behavioral inference is limited.","Players with identical contribution patterns may differ in unobserved traits (e.g., conditional strategies, occasional signaling) that we cannot detect from these rounds."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.46},{"player":"FROG","probability":0.44},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently contributed the full 20 coins every round (steady high contributions across all 10 rounds), matching Evelyn's reliable generosity.","No recorded punishment activity for MOOSE and FROG across the observed rounds, and only a single 1-unit punishment by SLOTH — consistent with Evelyn's tendency to rarely punish and to forgive lapses rather than police them.","None of these three players engaged in large or repeated punitive actions (unlike many others), aligning with Evelyn's preference to model cooperative norms rather than enforce them through punishment."],"uncertainty_notes":["We cannot observe rewarding behavior (Evelyn sometimes uses small costly rewards), so matches are based only on contribution and punishment logs.","Multiple players contributed very consistently; the main distinguishing signal is punishment activity, which may still miss other cooperative behaviors (e.g., communication or prosocial signaling) not available in this dataset.","Assignment is probabilistic because a few other high-contributing players differ only slightly in punishment frequency, so some ambiguity remains about which best captures subtle motivational differences."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently contributed the full pot after an initial accidental lapse, apologized and used encouraging, norm-promoting messages (urging cooperation, thanking teammates) — matches Evelyn’s tendency to model cooperative norms and forgive mistakes.","Gorilla contributed the full pot from the first round onward, matching Evelyn’s baseline of reliably high contributions, but showed little of Evelyn’s communicative, norm-supporting style.","Chicken contributed the full pot throughout, matching Evelyn’s steady high contributions, but had minimal social messaging so aligns less on the ‘modeling norms’/forgiving communicator aspects."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observations are limited to binary contribute/withdraw choices and public chat; internal motives (moral duty vs. payoff maximization) aren’t directly observable.","DUCK’s initial zero contribution was explained as a control error — could be genuine mistake or strategic signaling; I assume it was accidental based on the chat.","Players could be role-playing or responding to experiment framing; small-sample chat behavior may not fully reflect deeper personality." ]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.75},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.20},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["GORILLA frequently contributed at or near the maximum (many 20s) across rounds and repeatedly paid small costly rewards to multiple teammates — a pattern of steady high contribution plus small positive incentives consistent with Evelyn’s norm‑modeling generosity.","CHICKEN often gave small rewards (especially to GORILLA) and showed episodes of high contribution, indicating a willingness to support cooperators; however CHICKEN’s contributions are more variable (many 0s intermixed with 20s), making them a weaker match than GORILLA.","SLOTH contributed moderately often (many 10s) and occasionally rewarded, which fits Evelyn’s preference for modeling norms more than punishing, but SLOTH’s frequent zeros and low reward frequency make them less consistent with the profile of reliably high giving."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe revealed actions (contributions and rewards) but not private payoffs, intentions, or how players responded to unobserved information; similar surface behavior can arise from different motives.","Endgame effects and strategic adjustments across 29 rounds may distort patterns (e.g., some players cut contributions or rewards late in the game), complicating attribution to a stable prosocial identity.","Reward frequency and magnitude are visible but do not fully capture off‑record forgiveness or long‑term commitment that define Evelyn’s stated motivations, so the match is probabilistic rather than definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.8},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH contributed 20 in nearly every round (two small lapses), matching a steady, high-contribution, normative cooperator.","No punishments occurred at all, which fits Evelyn’s tendency to rarely punish and to model cooperation rather than police it.","GORILLA often contributed high amounts but showed repeated mid-level drops (10s) — somewhat cooperative but less consistently altruistic than SLOTH.","DUCK mostly contributed low amounts and frequently defected, inconsistent with Evelyn’s reliably high, selfless contributions."],"uncertainty_notes":["No punishment actions were observed, so we cannot directly assess willingness to punish or to use small costly rewards.","Contributions alone may reflect strategic responses to expectations rather than intrinsic moral motivation; occasional low contributions (e.g., SLOTH’s two 5s) could be noise or strategic moves.","We don’t observe off-game motivations, communication, or nonmonetary rewards that Evelyn might use to sustain cooperation, limiting confidence in alignment."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.7},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.2},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["DUCK is the only player who used small, costly rewards (two 1-unit rewards) and otherwise contributed near-maximum values — consistent with a strategy of steady giving plus occasional small rewards to sustain cooperation, and with forgiving/trusting responses to lapses.","GORILLA contributed 20 almost every round and engaged in social encouragement/scolding, matching the strong cooperative norm adherence in the profile but showing more policing than the profile’s preference for modeling over punishment.","CHICKEN consistently contributed the maximum throughout, matching the steady-contribution aspect of the profile, but showed no use of rewards and no communicative forgiveness signals, making them a weaker match than DUCK."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only two reward actions were observed (both from DUCK); absence of punishments could reflect group norms or experimental context, not individual preference.","Some low contributions (zeros, a 13, and a 10) may reflect misclicks or one-off mistakes (e.g., DOG apologized), so inferred motives could be noisy.","We do not observe private intentions or off-record communications; matching is based on revealed contributions, two small rewards, and chat messages only."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.55},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.30},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["DUCK: High contribution rate (6/8 rounds) and frequent, targeted reward giving (including occasional larger gifts) — matches Evelyn's steady giving plus occasional costly rewards to sustain cooperation.","SLOTH: Highest contribution consistency (7/8 rounds), reflecting Evelyn's tendency to reliably contribute and prioritize collective welfare, though SLOTH rarely gave rewards in the observed rounds.","GORILLA: Moderate contributor (5/8) who repeatedly gave small rewards to others, consistent with Evelyn's use of small rewarding to reinforce cooperation, but slightly less consistent in contributing than DUCK or SLOTH."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only eight rounds observed — behavior could shift with more rounds.","Rewards sometimes go to noncontributors (ambiguous motives), so inferring intent (sustaining cooperation vs. other motives) is uncertain.","Chat and unobserved off-stage reasoning not available, so matching is based solely on contributions and reward patterns."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DOG","probability":0.70},{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["DOG contributed in every observed round (consistent high contribution), sent a pro-cooperation chat, and did not punish others — matches Evelyn’s steady, trusting, non-punitive cooperative style.","MOOSE contributed in most rounds and advocated full contribution in chat, showing steady generosity and norm promotion; made a small number of punishments (minor policing), which is close but less consistent with Evelyn’s preference to avoid punishment.","CHICKEN also contributed frequently but repeatedly used costly punishment against defectors, which conflicts with Evelyn’s tendency to rarely punish; included as a distant match because of high contribution rate. "],"uncertainty_notes":["Only eight rounds observed; some players might use rewards (Evelyn sometimes rewards) but no positive rewards were sent in these rounds, limiting detection of Evelyn-like rewarding behavior.","Punishment actions may reflect momentary frustration or norm enforcement rather than stable preference; motives (forgiveness vs. policing) are not directly observable.","Chat content is limited and players’ private utilities or offscreen intentions are unknown, so matches are probabilistic rather than certain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.75},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.20},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["GORILLA: Always contributed the full 20, repeatedly gave small +1 rewards to sustain cooperators (especially SLOTH), and only rarely applied small punishments — closely matching Evelyn’s pattern of steady contribution and occasional small costly rewards to support cooperation.","SLOTH: Always contributed the full 20 (matches Evelyn’s reliable high contributions) but used relatively large punishments (e.g., -3, -2) and no rewards, which conflicts with Evelyn’s tendency to rarely punish and to prefer modeling norms over policing them.","DUCK: After an initial defection (round 1), Duck cooperated in all subsequent rounds and did not punish or reward; the one early defection makes Duck less consistent with Evelyn’s described steady generosity and trust."],"uncertainty_notes":["Cannot observe motives or private messages — rewards/punishments might reflect personal relationships or signaling rather than norm-supporting behavior.","Punishment/reward anonymity and aggregated summaries limit inference about who targeted whom intentionally and whether rewards were meant to sustain cooperation or for other reasons.","Conclusions are based on a single observed group and six rounds; behavior could vary across contexts or be one-off responses to specific events."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["DUCK contributed 20 from the very first round, repeatedly advocated ‘all in’, never punished others, and consistently supported group cooperation — matching Evelyn’s steady, high contributions and norm-modeling.","PARROT started slightly lower but quickly shifted to full contributions, voiced concern about group payoffs and coordination (e.g. asking to ‘wait’ when points fell), and did not punish — consistent with trusting, cooperative, forgiving behavior.","GORILLA contributed near-max early (15 then 20s), supported the all-in strategy in chat, and did not engage in punishment — aligning with reliably high contribution and low punishment tendencies."],"uncertainty_notes":["Can only observe public actions (contributions, punishments, chat); internal motives (altruism vs. strategic play) are not directly observable.","Later-round unanimous all-in behavior could reflect conditional cooperation enforced by others rather than pure intrinsic generosity.","Some players had one-off deviations (e.g. initial rounds or Rabbit’s single 0) that could reflect mistakes or experimental uncertainty rather than stable preferences; this complicates exact identification."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"COW","probability":0.6},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.25},{"player":"MONKEY","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["COW contributed the maximum (20) in every observed round — matches Evelyn’s reliable high contributions and willingness to underperform financially for group welfare.","COW repeatedly paid small, positive rewards to many peers across rounds, consistent with Evelyn’s tendency to use small costly rewards to sustain cooperation rather than punish.","PARROT shows consistently moderate-to-high contributions and frequently gives small, targeted rewards to cooperators — fits Evelyn’s cooperative generosity and norm-modeling.","MONKEY contributed high and consistently, matching Evelyn’s steady cooperative contributions, though Monkey sent few observable rewards (Evelyn would sometimes reward, but not necessarily every round)."],"uncertainty_notes":["We observe only contributions and costly reward actions; we cannot observe uncostly signaling, verbal intentions, or private motives that could distinguish personalities.","Reward recipients are anonymous in summaries and some players (e.g., CHICK vs CHICKEN) have similar names — this can obscure patterns of who is rewarding whom and why.","The sample is limited to nine rounds and 15 players; occasional deviations (e.g., short-term drops in contribution) may reflect strategic responses rather than dispositional differences, so match probabilities are approximate."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"OWL","probability":0.7},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.2},{"player":"DOG","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["OWL free-rode (0 contribution) in rounds 1–3, suggesting a clear short-term payoff focus and willingness to defect when profitable — a core Marcus trait.","OWL engaged little cooperative signaling to avoid exploitation and then changed behavior after being punished, consistent with an optimization response to incentives.","SLOTH displayed minimal chat, consistent behavior, and no punishments — aligns with Marcus’s tendency to ignore chat and follow a disciplined, instruction-focused strategy.","DOG had very little chat and stable contributions, matching Marcus’s low-interaction, payoff-focused style even if Dog did not visibly free-ride in this trace."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe actions (contributions, punishments, chat) and must infer motives; identical actions can reflect different motives (principled cooperation vs. calculated cooperation).","Several players show mixed behaviors (e.g., OWL both defected and punished others), making exact alignment uncertain.","The sample contains few clear final-round defections (Marcus is described as defecting in known final rounds), so matching is based primarily on early-round free-riding and low-chat behavior rather than a perfect match on all described traits."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.7},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.25},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["DUCK defects in the known final round (round 6 = 0), matching a payoff-maximizer who 'defects aggressively in known final rounds'.","DUCK shows low-to-moderate contributions earlier (10,10,15,15) then a late-round spike and final defection—consistent with someone who contributes when it boosts interim return but abandons cooperation at the end to maximize private payoff.","GORILLA exhibits opportunistic free-riding (round 5 = 0) and generally low/mixed contributions, which partially matches a self-interested strategy though Gorilla’s final-round cooperation (20) contradicts the strong end-game defection expectation.","SLOTH’s constant full contributions (always 20) are inconsistent with the described self-interested, exploitative profile and thus a poor match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only contributions are observed; no chat or stated intentions to confirm motives.","Players could be conditional cooperators, experimenting, or responding to perceived norms rather than straightforward payoff maximizers.","Small number of rounds (6) and noisy mid-game variation allow alternative interpretations (e.g., mistakes, reciprocity attempts), so match probabilities are approximate."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.17},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.08}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH: contributed 20 early then sharply reduced to 0 in round 5 and only 10 in final round — pattern consistent with opportunistic free-riding and endgame defection typical of a payoff-maximizer.","DUCK: low contributions early (6), then moderate (10) and a single punishment action — the low contributions fit self-interested payoff-seeking, but the use of costly punishment is inconsistent with a strict payoff-maximizer who seldom punishes.","GORILLA: always contributed 20 every round and never punished — consistent with a cooperative/other-regarding type, not with the self-interested Marcus profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral motives are inferred solely from contributions and one observed punishment; the same sequence can reflect conditional cooperation, experiment, or coordination rather than pure self-interest.","A single punishment by DUCK could be an anomaly, mistake, or strategic signal rather than a stable preference for costly punishment; this increases uncertainty in assigning probabilities.","SLOTH’s mid-game cooperation then drop could reflect strategic concealment or conditional responses to perceived history; with only six rounds and no chat context, certainty is limited."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.75},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["MOOSE: contributed early but then stopped contributing for many consecutive later rounds (persistent 0s in rounds 13–26) and never reversed before the game ended — consistent with a strategy of appearing cooperative early then free‑riding/defecting once endgame incentives dominate.","GORILLA: several early zero contributions and opportunistic shifts; also engaged in targeted punishment later. That mixed, self‑interested pattern (initial free‑rides, then selective punitive moves) aligns moderately with a payoff‑maximizer who uses punishment instrumentally.","PARROT: multiple early non‑contributions and frequent punitive actions toward others — indicative of someone willing to sacrifice to influence others’ behavior or protect private payoff. Less close than MOOSE because PARROT is more actively punitive (costly), while the prototype tends to avoid costly reciprocity)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral inference from contributions and public punish/reward logs is noisy: punishment/reward choices can reflect retaliation, coordination, or spite rather than pure payoff maximization.","Chat activity and some players’ visible helping/teaching (e.g., MOOSE asking questions, then rewarding small amounts) complicate attribution—those social signals might mask instrumental self‑interest or genuine confusion.","We cannot observe unlisted players or private payoffs; some players may have been randomly assigned or bots, which could alter apparent strategies. Probabilities reflect best fit to the observed contribution/punishment pattern, not certainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.5},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.2}],"behavioral_basis":["CHICKEN and DUCK free-rode in the early rounds (rounds 1–3), which matches Marcus’s tendency to exploit cooperators when profitable.","CHICKEN had minimal/chat-light presence and a mistaken comment about thinking they had contributed, consistent with Marcus’s profile of ignoring chat and focusing on payoff optimization rather than social signaling.","GORILLA displays the economist-style calculation and explicit payoff reasoning (noting group payoffs and computing 300/74), aligning with Marcus’s instruction-reading, calculation-oriented mindset even though GORILLA actually cooperated."],"uncertainty_notes":["No observed player persistently defected late in the sequence (Marcus would likely defect whenever privately optimal, including end-game), so matches are imperfect.","Chat behavior and short messages could reflect personality or situational factors (e.g., confusion, joking) rather than stable strategic type.","Players’ private motives and whether contributions reflect mixed strategies cannot be observed; inferences are based only on the limited contribution/chat signals shown."]}
